q: What schema design choices make a Unity Catalog table Genie-friendly vs Genie-hostile?
a: Genie generates SQL from UC metadata — table names, column names, descriptions, and declared relationships. Schema design is therefore the accuracy floor you can't overcome with Space configuration. Genie-friendly patterns: (1) Table and column descriptions are non-negotiable — every column Genie might reference needs a description that explains what it contains, its unit, and any business rules (e.g. 'order_total: total invoice value in USD, including tax, excluding refunds'). Ambiguous names like 'status' or 'created_at' without descriptions cause consistent wrong answers. (2) Explicit relationship declarations — define primary key and foreign key constraints in Unity Catalog. Genie respects them to determine join cardinality; without them it may produce fan-out joins that double-count measures. Always specify whether a relationship is one-to-many or many-to-many to prevent row explosion. (3) Fewer, wider tables — aim for 5 or fewer tables in a Space, maximum 30. Pre-join commonly queried combinations into views or metric views before adding to the Space; forcing Genie to discover multi-hop join paths increases error rate. (4) Metric views — Unity Catalog metric views pre-define measures, dimensions, and aggregations in a governed object; they're particularly effective as the single source of business metrics in a Space. Genie-hostile patterns: deeply normalized schemas requiring 6+ joins, columns with cryptic abbreviations and no descriptions, many-to-many relationships without bridge tables, and numeric codes stored as integers without a lookup join.
fj: Genie is an AI — it'll figure out the joins from a normalized 3NF schema, I just need to add it to the Space.
fs: Genie generates SQL from UC metadata, not by reasoning over your ERD. Missing column descriptions, undeclared FK/PK relationships, and deep normalization are accuracy anti-patterns. Pre-join into views, declare relationships, and comment every column Genie touches.
